coredata.dc:description: Oilfield tracers are essential chemical additives in the petroleum extraction process, aiding in understanding reservoir flow characteristics, optimizing production strategies, and ultimately increasing oil recovery rates. Carbon dots (CDs), known for their tunable photoluminescent properties, ease of detection, and low toxicity, are considered promising candidates for the next generation of oilfield tracers. However, aqueous-phase oilfield tracers require CDs to possess good water solubility, high-temperature and high-salinity tolerance, low rock adsorption and detection limit, which renders many CDs unqualified for use. Currently, reports on CDs oilfield tracers are limited, with most being restricted to blue fluorescence, which has weak resistance to background fluorescence interference. In this work, by utilizing the rich organic content (17.5 wt%) of low-value lignite as a precursor, lignite-derived carbonized polymer dots (L-CPDs) exhibit green fluorescence with an absolute quantum yield (QY) of 19.78%. Comprehensive evaluations show L-CPDs offer good salt tolerance, thermal stability, and pH stability, with a detection limit reaching 1 ppb and no significant cytotoxicity. Additionally, the tracing behavior of L-CPDs is demonstrated using a microfluidic chip, highlighting their potential as alternatives to existing tracer products.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and characterizations of L-CPDs. (a) Schematic illustration of the synthesis procedure of L-CPDs. (b) TEM image of L-CPDs. (c) HR-TEM image of L-CPDs, the inset is the 2D FFT image. (d) The particle size distribution of L-CPDs from (b). (e) 2D and (f) 3D AFM images of L-CPDs. (g) Height statistics of L-CPDs. (h) The Raman spectra of L-CPDs. (i) The FT-IR spectra of L-CPDs. (j) The fluorescence spectra of L-CPDs, excited at 365 nm. The inset shows a fluorescent image of the L-CPDs.

Fluorescence behavior and elemental analysis of L-WOM and L-CPDs. (a) Emission peaks and absolute QY of the products at different temperatures. (b) Time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of L-WOM and L-CPDs. 3D scan fluorescence spectrum of the product at (c) 60 °C, (d) 140 °C, (e) 220 °C, (f) 320 °C. (g) The FT-IR spectrum of L-WOM. The XPS high-resolution spectrum of L-WOM (h) C1s, (i) N1s. The XPS high-resolution spectrum of L-CPDs (j) C1s, (k) O1s, (l) N1s.

The synthesis mechanism of L-CPDs and possible precursor structures. (a) Schematic of the synthesis mechanism of L-CPDs. (b) Thymidine. (c) Oxyphyllol B. (d) 2,3-dehydrosilybin. (e) Esculentic acid. (f) 5-hydroxyxanthotoxin. (g) Cyclo(Tyr-Phe). (h) Possible formation mechanism of the sp2-carbon core structure.

Comprehensive evaluation of L-CPDs performance as oilfield tracers. (a) Optical observation of salt tolerance, high-temperature stability and compatibility with crude oil. (b) Statistical analysis of fluorescence intensity and emission peaks for all tested samples in (a). (c) The LOD of L-CPDs in API brine. (d) 3D scanning fluorescence spectra of L-CPDs solutions at pH values of 5, 7, and 9. (e) Cytotoxicity assessment of L-CPDs. (f) Comparison of the performance between L-CPDs and other CDs oilfield tracers.

Simulation experiment of L-CPDs tracer. (a) Optical photographs of the microfluidic chip, 8.7 cm in length and 6.5 cm in width. (b) Photograph of the microfluidic chip under UV light (365 nm) before the displacement. Photographs of the microfluidic chip under 365 nm showing the fluorescent tracer reaching the outlet, (c) blue tracer breakthrough at 4 min 40 s, (d) orange tracer breakthrough at 9 min, (e) green tracer (L-CPDs) breakthrough at 17 min. (f) Photograph of the microfluidic chip under 365 nm at 70 min when the injection of fluorescent tracers stopped. (g) Illustration of the simulated tracer test in a classical 9-spot well pattern. (h) The volumetric and areal sweep efficiencies of three types of oilfield tracers. (i) Fluorescence spectra of the fluids measured at each breakthrough time. Cumulative weight of L-CPDs. (j) Fluorescence intensity of L-CPDs in the sampling solution over time. (k) Cumulative weight of L-CPDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Lignite-derived carbonized polymer dots for oilfield tracers

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Oilfield tracers are essential chemical additives in the petroleum extraction process, aiding in understanding reservoir flow characteristics, optimizing production strategies, and ultimately increasing oil recovery rates. Carbon dots (CDs), known for their tunable photoluminescent properties, ease of detection, and low toxicity, are considered promising candidates for the next generation of oilfield tracers. However, aqueous-phase oilfield tracers require CDs to possess good water solubility, high-temperature and high-salinity tolerance, low rock adsorption and detection limit, which renders many CDs unqualified for use. Currently, reports on CDs oilfield tracers are limited, with most being restricted to blue fluorescence, which has weak resistance to background fluorescence interference. In this work, by utilizing the rich organic content (17.5 wt%) of low-value lignite as a precursor, lignite-derived carbonized polymer dots (L-CPDs) exhibit green fluorescence with an absolute quantum yield (QY) of 19.78%. Comprehensive evaluations show L-CPDs offer good salt tolerance, thermal stability, and pH stability, with a detection limit reaching 1 ppb and no significant cytotoxicity. Additionally, the tracing behavior of L-CPDs is demonstrated using a microfluidic chip, highlighting their potential as alternatives to existing tracer products.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All relevant data are within the manuscript and its additional files.

ce:section.ce:para: Oilfield tracers are commonly used chemical additives in the oil production process [1]. The procedure involves injecting tracers into injection wells and taking samples from surrounding production wells for analysis. Monitoring tracers in produced fluids can reveal well-to-well connectivity, water flow direction and velocity, volumetric sweep efficiency, reservoir heterogeneity, and remaining oil saturation [2,3]. This aids in understanding reservoir flow characteristics, optimizing production strategies, and enhancing oil recovery [4]. Initially, radioactive isotopes were used, but their high cost, complex operation, long analysis period, and associated hazards limited their application [5]. Currently, the main types of oilfield tracers include stable isotopes, inorganic salts, fluorescent dyes, and fluorobenzoic acids [5]. However, the synergy of low cost, high stability, low toxicity and ease of analysis cannot be achieved. For example, inorganic salts and fluorobenzoic acids require lab analysis using liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry, which takes hours or even days, not including sample shipping or transportation time from the field to the lab. Fluorescent dyes suffer from poor stability and susceptibility to adsorption, leading to high consumption and low detection accuracy [6–10]. Therefore, a cost-effective, stable oilfield tracer with low toxicity that can be analyzed in real-time is highly desirable.

Carbon dots (CDs), which exhibit tunable photoluminescent properties and high chemical stability, are considered promising candidates for the next generation of oilfield tracers [11]. On-site real-time monitoring and data analysis can be achieved using portable photometers, thereby providing timely information to the field engineers to make the optimal decisions for the oil production. Additionally, their tunable photoluminescent properties enable the customization of a wide range of tracers identifiable by the fluorescent spectroscopy, allowing them to address more complex reservoir conditions. There are numerous reports on the bottom-up synthesis of CDs; however, aqueous-phase oilfield tracers require CDs to possess water solubility, high-temperature and high-salinity tolerance, and low adsorption by rocks. These stringent requirements render many CDs unsuitable for use, resulting in a limited number of reported CDs oilfield tracers. Additionally, most reported CDs oilfield tracers exhibit blue fluorescence, which can be interfered with by the fluorescence of crude oil, potentially affecting the accuracy of detection results. Furthermore, these CDs oilfield tracers are predominantly synthesized directly from pure organic compounds, which presents limitations in cost control and yield [12–20]. Coal is regarded as an ideal carbon source, and the top-down synthesis of CDs using coal as the raw material is both economical and efficient [21,22]. However, CDs synthesized using top-down approaches from coal typically exhibit an initial absolute quantum yield (QY) of less than 10 % [11]. This low QY results in a high limit of detection (LOD) and poor detection accuracy, rendering them unsuitable for oilfield tracers. Although heteroatom doping, alkylation, esterification, imidization, passivation, and the introduction of fluorescent ligands can enhance QY, these post-treatment processes are complex and increase production costs, reducing their economic value significantly [23–26]. Therefore, it is essential to develop a cost-effective method for producing CDs with high QY and stability for their use as oilfield tracers.

Herein, a combined top-down and bottom-up method was proposed to synthesize carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) from lignite. Firstly, lignite-derived water-soluble organic matter (L-WOM) was attained through a top-down method via ultrasound-assisted OH* reactions. Subsequently, the L-WOM underwent a high-temperature bottom-up process, resulting in the formation of bright green fluorescent lignite-derived CPDs (L-CPDs). The performance of L-CPDs as oilfield tracers was extensively evaluated, focusing on their salt tolerance, high-temperature stability, compatibility with crude oil, pH stability, and cytotoxicity. A quantitative detection model was developed based on the relationship between fluorescence intensity and concentration of L-CPDs. Finally, the tracing behavior of L-CPDs was demonstrated using a microfluidic chip.

Lignite was obtained from Zhundong Coal Mine in China. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%), ammonia monohydrate (NH3·H2O, 25-28%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 96%) and hydrogen chloride (HCl, 36-38%) were purchased from the Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China. Citric acid and ethylenediamine (EDA, 99%) were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Sodium chloride (NaCl), Calcium chloride (CaCl2), Rhodamine B, potassium chloride (KCl), polyacrylamide (PAM), and sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s Medium (DMEM), RPMI 1640 Medium, fetal bovine serum (FBS), Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8), penicillin-streptomycin, phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) were obtained from ThermoFisher. All reagents were used as received without any further purification. All aqueous solutions used throughout the experiment were prepared with deionized (DI) water.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images of L-CPDs were obtained on a JEOL JEM-F200 electron microscope at 200 kV. L-CPDs size distribution was measured by atomic force microscopy (AFM, Multimode8 SPM) operating at the tapping mode. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained on a Bruker VERTEX70 spectrograph. Raman scattering spectra were obtained using Horiba Jobin Yvon HR800 spectrometer with 325 nm laser. Fluorescence spectra and three-dimensional (3D) scan fluorescence spectra were recorded by a Hitachi F-4700 spectrophotometer. The FL3C-111 TCSPC instrument system (HORIBA, Japan) was used to measure the time-resolved fluorescence decay curve and photobleaching resistance under 365 nm excitation. The sample’s absolute QY was measured by the OmniFluo990 LSP instrument (Zolix, China) and an integrating sphere. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed with a Thermo Fisher ES-CALAB Xi+ electron spectrometer from VG Scientific using 400 W Al-Kα radiation. The thermal stability of the L-WOM was analyzed with a synchronous thermal analyzer (TGA/DSC 3+ ). The testing was carried out from 30 to 800 °C at a heating rate of 10 K min−1 under a nitrogen atmosphere. NMR was conducted on AVANCE III HD 600 MHz. 1H and 13C NMR spectra, 1H-1H homonuclear correlated spectroscopy (COSY), 1H-13C heteronuclear single quantum coherence (HSQC), 1H-13C heteronuclear multiple bond correlation (HMBC), 1H-15N HMBC spectra were analyzed with MestreNova software and referenced using the deuterium oxide (D2O) signal. Ultra-high performance liquid chromatography-quadrupole time-of-flight mass spectrometry (WATERS I-Class VION IMS QTof) was used to analyze the potential natural products present in L-WOM. The zeta potential of L-CPDs in DI water was measured using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZSE. The pH of the L-CPDs aqueous solution was adjusted by adding NaOH and HCl solutions.

Lignite sample (20 g) was poured into a 5 L beaker containing 2 L of H2O2. The beaker was placed in a sonicator at room temperature and sonicated at a frequency of 40 kHz for 6 h. After sonication, NH3·H2O was slowly added dropwise into the beaker to adjust the solution pH to 7. The resulting solution was first filtered through a 200 nm membrane, then filtered using a cross-flow ultrafiltration system equipped with a 500 Da membrane. The filtrate was subsequently concentrated using a cross-flow ultrafiltration system equipped with a 100 Da membrane, yielding the L-WOM solution. The L-WOM solution was subsequently dried at 60 °C to obtain 3.5 g L-WOM powder. For the comparison sample, filtration was conducted using only a 500 Da membrane, resulting in a filtrate that is the unpurified L-WOM solution.

The obtained 3.5 g L-WOM powder was placed in a 300 mL crucible and heated at 320 °C for 2 h in air. After the temperature naturally cooled to room temperature, the carbonized product was washed and filtered using a 200 nm membrane. The filtrate was then subjected to secondary filtration using a 3 kD membrane, followed by concentration with a 500 Da membrane to obtain the L-CPDs concentrate. The L-CPDs concentrate was then freeze-dried to obtain 0.41 g of L-CPDs powder. For comparative samples, products were heated for 2 h at temperatures of 60 °C, 100 °C, 140 °C, 180 °C, 220 °C, 260 °C, 300 °C, 310 °C, 330 °C, 340 °C, and 350 °C, while keeping all other conditions constant.
The blue fluorescent CDs was prepared with the reported method [27].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Salt tolerance and High-Temperature stability Tests
ce:section.ce:para: American Petroleum Institute (API) brine (8 wt% NaCl and 2 wt% CaCl2) was prepared according to the standards. A 50 ppm aqueous solution of L-CPDs and the brine solution were separately prepared and allowed to stand at room temperature for 7 days to test the salt tolerance of L-CPDs. Additionally, a 50 ppm L-CPDs brine solution was prepared again, sealed, and placed at 200 °F for 2 h. After natural cooling to room temperature, the sample was taken out to test the high-temperature stability of L-CPDs. A brine solution with total dissolved solids (TDS) = 300000 ppm was prepared and used as a solvent to test the stability of L-CPDs under ultra-high salinity conditions. 3 mL of a proprietary carboxylated polyether-based dispersant was added to a 50 mL brine solution containing 50 ppm of L-CPDs. The sample was sealed and kept at 122 °F for 2 h. After naturally cooling to room temperature, the fluorescence spectrum of the sample was tested under 365 nm excitation. Additionally, a 50 mL L-CPDs brine solution (concentration of 50 ppm, without dispersant) was prepared as a control sample to compare its fluorescence performance. The fluorescence spectra of 50 ppm L-CPDs in API brine were measured after being stored for 24 h at different temperatures to investigate the long-term photothermal stability of L-CPDs.

A 50 ppm L-CPDs brine solution was mixed with crude oil at volumetric ratios of 3:1, 1:1, and 1:3, respectively, for compatibility testing. Each sample was vigorously shaken and then allowed to stand at 200 °F for 2 h. After naturally cooling to room temperature, samples were retrieved for analysis. Using DI water as the solvent and maintaining a constant concentration of L-CPDs at 20 ppm, the effects of three additives (KCl, PAM, and SDS), both individually and in combination, on the fluorescence characteristics of L-CPDs were investigated.

4 T1 cells were cultured in RPMI 1640 Medium and L929 cells were cultured in DMEM. All the cells were grown in the media which supplied with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS), 100 U mL−1 penicillin and 100 μg mL−1 streptomycin in a humidified incubator at 37 °C with 5% CO2 and subcultured every one or two days.

The CCK-8 assays were used to evaluate the cytotoxicity of L-CPDs. 4 T1 and L929 cells were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 6000-8000 cells per well. After overnight culture, each well was replaced with 100 μL of fresh medium containing L-CPDs with different concentrations. After 24 h of incubation, CCK-8 reagent was added to each well. The UV absorption was recorded using a plate reader (Perkin-Elmer Victor3t) at 450 nm. Each trial was performed with 5 wells in parallel.

A glass microfluidic chip, measuring 8.7 cm in length and 6.5 cm in width, with a pore height of 500 μm and a pore volume of 0.7 mL, featuring 4 inlets/outlets, was developed. The experimental procedure was divided into three parts to simulate the tracing behavior of fluorescent tracers. Initially, the chip was saturated with API brine solution. Subsequently, it was configured with 3 inlets and 1 outlet. Simultaneous injections of blue fluorescent CDs brine solution, orange fluorescent dye (Rhodamine B) brine solution, and green fluorescent L-CPDs brine solution were conducted at a flow rate of 0.01 mL min−1 each, lasting for 70 min. Throughout this process, the chip was continuously irradiated with a UV lamp (365 nm) and recorded using a Samsung Galaxy Note 10+ smartphone to monitor the tracing behavior of the three fluorescent tracers. Finally, the brine was reinjected into the three inlets at 0.01 mL min−1 to flush out any remaining fluorescent tracers in the chip, continuing for 140 min. Samples were periodically collected from the outlet during the entire process. Samples were collected every 3-5 min, and each sample solution was diluted to a final volume of 4 mL for analysis.

The synthesis procedure of L-CPDs is illustrated in Fig. 1 (a). Due to the lower degree of coalification in lignite, it is rich in polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons and aliphatic compounds [28]. These organic matters are linked through various types of bridging bonds and alkyl chains, collectively forming a 3D network of organic matter in lignite [29]. Hence, with the assistance of ultrasonic, OH* radicals generated by H2O2 can easily penetrate the internal structure of lignite, targeting weaker bridging bonds such as −O−, –CH2−O−, –CH2−CH2−, thus disrupting the organic network of lignite and yielding a solution derived from lignite [30]. It is worth noting that this solution also contains nano-sized graphite-like microcrystals, which are considered quasi-CDs [31]. This method of obtaining CDs directly from precursors is regarded as a top-down approach [31]. CDs synthesized from coal using a top-down approach generally have an initial absolute QY of less than 10%. The lower fluorescence intensity results in a higher LOD, which does not meet the application requirements for oilfield tracers. Therefore, based on this, a combined top-down and bottom-up method is proposed to synthesize CPDs from lignite.

A cross-flow ultrafiltration technique was employed to isolate L-WOM, with molecular weights ranging from 100 Da-500 Da. Clearly, L-WOM represents an assembly of abundant water-soluble organic molecules. At 320 °C, these organic molecules undergo crosslinking, polymerization, and carbonization, gradually forming L-CPDs composed of sp2-carbon cores and sp3-carbon shells. Based on the mass of 20 g of raw lignite, the mass of L-WOM is 3.5 g, with a yield of 17.5%; the mass of L-CPDs is 0.41 g, with a yield of 2%. Optical photographs of L-WOM and L-CPDs powders are shown in Fig. S1, where L-WOM appears reddish-brown, and L-CPDs appear turmeric yellow.

TEM image of L-CPDs is shown in Fig. 1(b), exhibiting a quasi-spherical morphology. The HR-TEM image in Fig. 1(c) shows that the L-CPDs particles have a well-crystallized carbon core with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphite, which is characteristic of the sp2-carbon core structure typically found in CPDs [32,33]. Additionally, around the sp2-carbon cores, HR-TEM image clearly shows the amorphous carbon structure of L-CPDs, namely the sp3-carbon shell. Through statistical analysis, the average particle size of L-CPDs is determined to be 3.43 ± 0.01 nm (Fig. 1d). The two-dimensional (2D) and 3D AFM images of L-CPDs demonstrate a relatively uniform particle distribution (Fig. 1e and f); height statistical analysis reveals an average height of 2.79 ± 0.03 nm (Fig. 1g), which closely aligns with the TEM statistical results (Fig. 1d), confirming the quasi-spherical morphology of L-CPDs particles.

Raman spectrum was employed for further analysis of sp2- and sp3-carbon structures. As shown in Fig. 1(h), the D band and G band of L-CPDs are located near 1360 cm−1 and 1606 cm−1, respectively, indicating the presence of sp2- and sp3-hybridized structures [34]. The sharp and prominent G peak of L-CPDs indicates a high degree of graphitization and good crystallinity in the carbon core. The relatively high ID/IG value (0.65) suggests a significant presence of amorphous carbon, reflecting a higher content of sp3-carbon, which originates from the amorphous carbon shell of L-CPDs. Combined with the observations from HR-TEM, the core-shell structure of L-CPDs is thoroughly confirmed [35]. FT-IR was employed to analyze the surface functional groups of L-CPDs. As shown in Fig. 1(i), the peak at 3423 cm−1 is caused by stretching vibrations of O-H, while the shoulder peaks at 1722 cm−1 and 1082 cm−1 are assigned to C=O and C−O, respectively [36]. Hence, the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups such as –OH and –COOH on the surface of L-CPDs ensures their good water solubility. The strong peak observed at 1593 cm- 1 corresponds to C=C, [37] indicating that L-CPDs possess larger-sized conjugated sp2-carbon structures. Another strong peak at 1396 cm−1 can be attributed to C−N, while the peak at 3217 cm−1 can be assigned to N−H, indicating that L-CPDs are nitrogen (N)-doped, which is closely related to their fluorescence properties [38]. The fluorescence spectrum of L-CPDs is illustrated in Fig. 1(j). In DI water, with an excitation wavelength of 365 nm, the maximum emission peak of L-CPDs at a concentration of 0.03 mg mL−1 is observed at 519 nm, exhibiting bright green fluorescence (inset). The absolute QY of L-CPDs measured by integrating sphere is 19.78% (Fig. S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent properties and synthesis mechanism of L-CPDs
ce:section.ce:para: L-CPDs were synthesized by directly heating L-WOM under air; hence, temperature is the primary influencing factor in this process. To investigate the fluorescence behavior of L-CPDs, the fluorescence characteristics of the products at different temperatures were discussed first, as shown in Fig. 2 (a). At 60 °C, the product exhibited a blue emission, with a maximum emission peak at 452 nm and an absolute QY of 5.23%. With increasing temperature, the products showed emission redshift and an increase in QY. At 320 °C, the product exhibited the maximum redshift, displaying green emission at 519 nm; the absolute QY reached a maximum of 19.78%. As the temperature continued to rise, the products showed emission blueshift and a decrease in QY. At 350 °C, the product exhibited the maximum blueshift emission at 424 nm, displaying deep blue emission; the absolute QY was 6.6%. It is evident that L-CPDs prepared at 320 °C exhibit the optimal fluorescence performance, with an absolute QY reaching 19.78% and the maximum emission peak at 519 nm. It should be noted that the L-CPDs described in this work specifically refer to the product prepared at 320 °C. Time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of L-WOM and L-CPD are shown in Fig. 2(b), with an average fluorescence lifetime of 5.00 ns for L-WOM and 8.17 ns for L-CPDs. To provide a more intuitive illustration of the effect of temperature on the fluorescence properties of the products, Fig. 2(c)-(f) respectively present the 3D scanned fluorescence spectra of the products prepared at 60 °C, 140 °C, 220 °C, and 320 °C. Clearly, the sample prepared at 60 °C exhibits only one fluorescence center in the blue region. As the preparation temperature increases, corresponding products begin to show a second fluorescence center, gradually shifting towards the green region, until at 320 °C, the fluorescence center of the product completely shifts to the green region. The shift in fluorescence centers due to temperature variation reflects the transformation of L-WOM as a precursor through stages of small molecule polymerization, cross-linking, and nucleation, ultimately leading to the formation of L-CPDs [39]. At 60 °C, the relatively low temperature allows the product to retain the fluorescence characteristics of L-WOM, characterized by blue fluorescence centers originating from small molecules. As the temperature rises to 140 °C, small molecules begin to undergo dehydration-condensation and cross-linking, resulting in the emergence of green fluorescence centers. At this stage, the precursor is in a highly disordered state, so the maximum emission center remains in the blue region, still dominated by small molecules. With a further increase in temperature to 220 °C, the degree of cross-linking intensifies, leading to a denser and more stable structure, initiating nucleation, and the maximum emission center starts to shift to the green fluorescence region. As carbonization progresses and the temperature reaches 320 °C, L-CPDs are formed. At this point, larger sp2-conjugated carbon domains develop internally, while the external polymer content decreases, forming a sp3-carbon shell. Consequently, the fluorescence center fully shifts to the green region, dominated by the sp2-carbon core [33]. Interestingly, this process is hardly affected by the atmosphere, as depicted in the TGA curves in Fig. S3. Before 420 °C, the weight loss of L-WOM in both air and argon atmospheres is nearly identical. In other words, the oxygen in the air plays a minimal role in the formation of L-CPDs, indicating that L-CPDs originate from intermolecular reactions of L-WOM. This indirectly supports the proposed formation process of L-CPDs described above. The synthesis mechanism and fluorescence origin of L-CPDs are discussed through a detailed analysis of the elemental composition of L-WOM and L-CPDs. The FT-IR spectrum of L-WOM, as shown in Fig. 2(g), reveals peaks at 2993 cm−1 and 2821 cm−1, attributed to the symmetric stretching vibrations of –CH3 and –CH2− groups, respectively, [40] indicating the presence of alkyl chain structures in L-WOM molecules. In the FT-IR spectrum of L-CPDs (Fig. 1i), characteristic peaks of –CH3 or –CH2− groups are not observed, suggesting that under high-temperature conditions, the original alkyl chains undergo condensation and transform into an amorphous carbon shell. The peaks observed at 1755 cm−1 and 1695 cm−1 are attributed to C=O and C(O)O stretching vibrations, respectively, [41] while the peaks at 1348 cm−1 and 1085 cm−1 are assigned to C−O−C and C−O stretching vibrations, respectively [42,43].

The abundance of oxygen-containing functional groups imparts good water solubility and reactivity to L-WOM. The peak observed at 3182 cm−1 is attributed to N-H stretching vibrations, while the peak at 1404 cm−1 is assigned to C−N stretching vibrations [38]. This confirms that the N in L-CPDs originates from the organic matter in lignite rather than residual NH3·H2O solution (which is used to neutralize excess H2O2). Additionally, the peak observed at 1550 cm−1 corresponds to C=C stretching vibrations, [37] originating from polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons present in L-WOM. Based on the characterization results of surface functional groups, it can be inferred that the possible reactions occur during the transformation from L-WOM to L-CPDs. For instance, dehydration condensation between COOH and –NH2 forms amide structures, and Schiff base reactions between C(O)O and –NH2 [44]. These are all classical reactions in the bottom-up synthesis of CDs. Compared to L-WOM, the FT-IR spectrum of L-CPDs (Fig. 1i) exhibits a reduction in the types and content of oxygen-containing functional groups, which further supports this inference.

As shown in Fig. S4(a), the XPS survey spectrum reveals that the surface of L-WOM contains three elements: C, O, and N, accounting for 60.38%, 32.31%, and 7.31%, respectively. It is worth noting that, to eliminate the influence of impurities such as minerals in lignite on the synthesis of L-CPDs, the molecular weight of L-WOM is greater than 100 Da. As shown in Fig. S4(b), the XPS survey spectrum of the unpurified L-WOM displays various elements, including S, Na, Si, and K, in addition to C, O, and N. Detailed elemental proportions are provided in Table S1. Interestingly, organic S is often found in coal, yet S is absent in L-WOM. Hence, the presence of S may be in the form of elemental S or sulfates and sulfites, which are eventually filtered out. As shown in Fig. 2(h), the high-resolution spectrum of C1s can be subdivided into five peaks at 284.8 eV (C−C/C=C), 286.8 eV (C−OH), 288.7 eV (C−N), 293 eV (O=C−N), and 295.9 eV (N−C=N), respectively [45–47]. The high-resolution spectrum of N1s is shown in Fig. 2(i), with peaks observed at 398.7 eV (pyridinic N), 399.7 eV (pyrrolic N), 400.6 eV (amine N) and 401.7 eV (graphitic N) [48–50]. The percentages of different bonding compositions calculated by XPS analysis are listed in Table S2. Overall, the XPS analysis results are highly consistent with the FT-IR characterization results, further validating the synthesis mechanism inference of L-CPDs. The XPS survey spectrum of L-CPDs is shown in Fig. S5, indicating the presence of three elements, namely C, O, and N, with relative atomic percentages of 57.43%, 34.58%, and 7.99%, respectively. Fig. 2(j) and (k) present the high-resolution C 1s and O 1s spectra, respectively, with peak positions observed at 284.8 eV (C−C/C=C), 286.6 eV (C−OH), 288.7 eV (C−N), 531.6 eV (C=O), and 533.1 eV (C−O) [51]. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum is shown in Fig. 2(l), revealing only two peaks at 399.2 eV (pyrrolic N) and 401.3 eV (graphitic N) [50]. Compared to L-WOM, the elemental composition of L-CPDs shows little variation, but the bonding composition has significantly decreased, possibly attributable to the carbonization process. The specific bonding compositions in percentage for C, O, and N are detailed in Table S3. The reduction in the types of surface functional groups, especially oxygen-containing functional groups, effectively reduces non-radiative transitions, promoting an increase in QY [52]. Moreover, L-CPDs possess a stable core–shell structure, where the rigidity of the shell significantly constrains the conjugated carbon core and restricts molecular vibrations, further enhancing the QY [35]. Additionally, the significantly increased content of graphitic N in L-CPDs indicates that more N is incorporated into the sp2-carbon framework, effectively reducing the bandgap between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), leading to a redshift in the fluorescence of L-CPDs, ultimately exhibiting green fluorescence [53].

Based on the above analysis, the synthesis mechanism of L-CPDs is proposed, as illustrated in Fig. 3 (a). L-WOM serves as the precursor material, undergoing intermolecular condensation upon heating to form low-molecular-weight polymers. With increasing carbonization, these low-molecular-weight polymers further crosslink, with polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon structures conjugating to form sp2-carbon cores, while other polymer chains form sp3-carbon shells with abundant surface functional groups. To further elucidate the synthesis mechanism of L-CPDs, it is necessary to propose the specific molecular structures present in L-WOM. NMR characterization of L-WOM was conducted. Due to the complexity of L-WOM composition, only one organic molecular structural fragment was identified based on the test results (Fig. S6). Coal originates from the remains of plants and algae, with these plant materials decomposing to form the organic components found in coal [54]. Therefore, lignite, as a type of coal with a lower graphitization degree, retains a significant amount of decomposition products from plant materials within its tissue structure [55]. These products may be considered natural compounds. Additionally, the ultrasonic-assisted H2O2 oxidation method is relatively mild, where hydroxyl radicals can only attack weaker bonds. Consequently, certain natural products may be released from the lignite matrix. Based on the above theory, employing ultra-high performance liquid chromatography-quadrupole time-of-flight mass spectrometry to identify organic compounds in L-WOM and comparing them with natural products may help to determine the structures of some organic compounds in L-WOM.

The ultra-high performance liquid chromatography profile of L-WOM is illustrated in Fig. S7. As depicted in Fig. 3(b)-(g), by searching natural product databases, the following compounds may exist in L-WOM: thymidine, oxyphyllol B, 2,3-dehydrosilybin, esculentic acid, 5-hydroxyxanthotoxin, and cyclo(tyr-phe). The mass errors (mDa) of these compounds are all less than 5‰ (Table S4). It is noteworthy that only thymidine and cyclo(tyr-phe) contain N elements, which are the decomposition products of plant deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) and proteins, respectively. It is generally believed that the N in coal originates from the macromolecules in plants. Additionally, these compounds all exhibit certain water solubility, and their elemental compositions are roughly consistent with the FT-IR and XPS analysis results of L-WOM. Hence, they are likely the primary precursor molecules for L-CPDs. The possible formation mechanism of the sp2-carbon core structure is illustrated in Fig. 3(h). Under heating conditions, intermolecular dehydration occurs between hydroxyl and hydroxyl groups, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, and hydroxyl and amide groups, forming oligomers. As the reaction progresses, cross-linking between the oligomers occurs, followed by further dehydration, leading to the formation of aromatic clusters. With increasing carbonization, the sp2-carbon structure forms, diffuses outward, and eventually nucleates [56]. Simultaneously, various functional groups undergo passivation, resulting in the generation of graphitic N, pyrrolic N [56–58].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance evaluation of L-CPDs as oilfield tracers
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of L-CPDs as oilfield tracers was comprehensively evaluated, as shown in Fig. 4 . Fig. 4(a) presents the fluorescence stability test of L-CPDs. At a concentration of 50 ppm, L-CPDs maintained good dispersion without aggregation or precipitation under different conditions: in DI water at room temperature (RT), API brine at RT, and API brine at 200 °F. The fluorescence images consistently displayed bright green fluorescence, indicating that L-CPDs possess good salt tolerance and high-temperature stability. Additionally, the brine solution of L-CPDs demonstrated good compatibility with crude oil at 200 °F, without forming additional phases, indicating that L-CPDs do not cause emulsification or aggregation, thereby preventing reservoir damage [14]. As shown in Fig. 4(b), a quantitative analysis of the fluorescence intensity and emission peaks of L-CPDs under the aforementioned conditions was conducted. The fluorescence intensity of L-CPDs showed minimal variation across all conditions, with emission peak shifts of less than 10 nm, further demonstrating the good stability of L-CPDs. Furthermore, the fluorescence responses of L-CPDs in API brine solutions after being maintained at different temperatures for 24 h were tested (Fig. S8). Compared to 30 °C, L-CPDs retained over 85% of their fluorescence intensity at 95 °C, demonstrating excellent long-term photothermal stability. The salinity of API brine (TDS) is 110,000 ppm. In some cases, produced water with a high TDS up to 300,000 ppm is used in waterflooding treatments. This high salinity often causes the aggregation or quenching of CDs [59,60]. To improve the salt tolerance of L-CPDs, a proprietary carboxylated polyether-based dispersant can be added to aid in their dispersion through steric effects. As shown in Fig. S9, the fluorescence intensity of the L-CPDs solution significantly decreases at a TDS of 300,000 ppm, with the maximum fluorescence emission shifting to around 450 nm. However, after the dispersant is added, the fluorescence properties of L-CPDs are largely restored, with the fluorescence emission peak and intensity comparable to those in DI water or API brine. The fluorescence intensity of L-CPDs in API brine decreased with decreasing concentration (Fig. S10), demonstrating its potential for quantitative analysis. This was further validated by a quantitative detection model established based on the relationship between fluorescence intensity and concentration (Fig. S11). As shown in Fig. 4(c), the L-CPDs in API brine can achieve a LOD as low as 1 ppb, owing to their high QY. This low detection limit not only ensures more accurate test results but also reduces the required dosage, thereby saving costs. As illustrated in Fig. 4(d), at pH values of 5, 7, and 9, the 3D scanning fluorescence spectra of L-CPDs solutions exhibit nearly identical fluorescent centers. Fig. S12 depicts the variation of zeta potential and fluorescence intensity of a 50 ppm L-CPDs aqueous solution as a function of pH. At pH values above 3, L-CPDs exhibit a negative zeta potential, indicating repulsion from typically negatively charged porous media, thereby minimizing adsorption risks [61]. Moreover, pH has minimal impact on the fluorescence intensity of L-CPDs, aligning closely with Fig. 4(b), and remains relatively stable under weakly acidic and alkaline conditions. The independence of pH ensures that L-CPDs maintain their original fluorescence response and facilitate smooth operations under different pH scenarios. The cytotoxicity of L-CPDs was evaluated across different cell lines (4 T1, L929) using the CCK-8 assay [62]. After 48 h of incubation, L-CPDs demonstrated no significant inhibition of cell viability, even at concentrations containing L-CPDs up to 1000 ppm, highlighting the low or non-cytotoxic nature of the L-CPDs (Fig. 4e). As depicted in Fig. 4(f), the performance of L-CPDs and other CDs oilfield tracers was compared across five aspects: fluorescence emission, maximum operating temperature, stability in brine solution expressed by TDS, QY, and LOD. Further details can be found in Table S5 [12–20]. It is evident that the comprehensive performance of L-CPDs proposed in this work ranks at the forefront. Firstly, the fluorescence color of most CDs tracers is typically blue, which is highly susceptible to background interference (residual oil micro-droplets and natural water-soluble organic molecules often exhibit blue fluorescence during oil–water separation), posing challenges in low-concentration measurements [12]. In contrast, the bright green fluorescence of L-CPDs minimizes background fluorescence interference during the testing process, thereby ensuring the reliability of the detection results. Additionally, L-CPDs exhibit stable fluorescence characteristics, with a LOD reaching 1 ppb. The lower LOD not only reduces operational costs but also enhances testing accuracy. Furthermore, the good salt tolerance (TDS = 300,000 ppm), high-temperature stability (200 °F), and pH stability of L-CPDs enable them to withstand a broader range of complex reservoir conditions. The stable core-shell structure of L-CPDs, characterized by a rigid amorphous shell, effectively protects the fluorescent core, providing strong resistance to photobleaching (Fig. S13). This structural advantage minimizes the risk of fluorescence intensity reduction due to ultraviolet irradiation during ground testing, thereby ensuring the successful implementation of real-time field detection. Considering the practical application environment of L-CPDs as oilfield tracers, we further investigated the effects of three commonly used oilfield additives on the fluorescence properties of L-CPDs, including clay stabilizer (KCl), oil-displacing additive (PAM) and surfactant (SDS). The test results indicate that L-CPDs exhibit a highly stable fluorescence response, with minimal influence from the additives (Fig. S14 and Fig. S15). This further demonstrates the potential utility of L-CPDs as effective oilfield tracers. In addition to their attractive performance advantages, the synthesis of L-CPDs involves low-cost materials, reagents, and equipment, with environmental benefits, making them highly suitable for industrial production.

The tracing behavior of L-CPDs was elucidated using a microfluidic chip. As shown in Fig. 5 (a), the microfluidic chip is designed with three inlets and one outlet to simulate three water injection wells and an oil production well. At inlet 1, a solution of blue-fluorescence CDs (prepared according to the method reported) is injected [27]. Green-fluorescence L-CPDs is introduced to inlet 2, while an orange-fluorescence dye (Rhodamine B) is pumped into inlet 3. These three materials serve as three different fluorescent tracers and are simultaneously injected into the microfluidic chip at a flow rate of 0.01 mL min−1. The detailed experimental procedure is provided in the supporting information. Fig. 5(b)-(f) shows the fluorescence images of the microfluidic chip at 0 min (tracer injections start), 4 min 40 s (blue-fluorescence CDs breakthrough), 9 min (orange-fluorescence dye breakthrough), 17 min (green-fluorescence CDs breakthrough), and 70 min (tracer injections end), respectively. Apparently, all three tracers successfully break through the chip and prove the inter-well connectivity between each injection well and the production well. Inter-well connectivity is a prerequisite indicator for the adoption of waterflooding treatment during the enhanced oil recovery process. However, to maximize the oil recovery efficiency, more reservoir information is required to help make a better waterflooding decision, such as volumetric sweep efficiency, fractures in the reservoir, permeability and areal and vertical heterogeneity. Volumetric sweep efficiency is defined as the fraction of the reservoir's pore volume swept by the displacing fluid [63]. A high volumetric sweep efficiency is always preferred because of the high volume of the reservoir contacted by the injected fluid, resulting in a high oil recovery rate. Evidently, the blue-fluorescence CDs are the first to break through with velocity at 1.9 cm min−1, followed by orange-fluorescence tracer at 0.9 cm min−1, and L-CPDs at 0.6 cm min−1. Under the same injection rate and inter-well distance, Rhodamine B exhibits slower propagation velocity than blue-fluorescence CDs, indicating a more complex pore structure between inlet 3 and outlet with fewer directly connected large channels and more small fractures that contribute to the higher volumetric sweep efficiency. L-CPDs demonstrate the slowest velocity at 0.6 cm min−1, thus highlighting the homogeneity of the pore structure and low permeability between inlet 2 and the outlet. To further interpret the data received from the test and correlate it with the field test scenario, a classical 9-spot well pattern is adopted for demonstration, with each production well surrounded by 8 water injection wells [64]. The use of the microfluidic chip precisely simulates a portion of this pattern, as schematically depicted in Fig. 5(g). During oil recovery process, adjusting waterflooding and stimulation strategies promptly based on different tracer information is crucial. As shown in Fig. 5(h), the volumetric and areal sweep efficiencies of blue (injection well 1), yellow (injection well 3), and green (injection well 2) fluorescent tracers are 6.67%, 12.86%, 24.29% and 4.45%, 11.15%, 22.56%, respectively. For injection well 1, higher breakthrough velocity and low sweep efficiency reflect the heterogeneity of the formation and indicate the presence of large channels between the injection and production well, suggesting a high recovery rate at the initial waterflooding treatment but low efficiency once the oil in the large channels are recovered; a stimulation strategy is recommended to improve the homogeneity of the reservoir between injection well 1 and production well. For example, a diverting agent can be implemented to block the large channels and divert the fluids towards small fractures to increase the sweep efficiency. The oil production rate from injection well 2 is expected to be slower than injection wells 1 and 3 at the beginning, but the production period and the overall recovery efficiency should be the highest due to its high volumetric sweep efficiency. For injection well 3, large channels seem not to exist in the formation, but there might be some medium-sized channels present that contribute to the faster breakthrough velocity than injection well 2. Hydraulic fracturing or matrix acidizing treatment is recommended to increase the density of small fractures, thus improving the sweep efficiency.

It is worth noting that in the actual application of tracers, the components of the tracers in the produced fluid must be analyzed to obtain the hidden reservoir information they carry. Given that multiple tracers may be present in the produced fluid, the fluorescence response of L-CPDs was also evaluated under the interference of other fluorescent tracers. At 17 min, the L-CPDs reached the outlet, and the fluorescence spectrum of the sampled solution exhibited a distinct shoulder peak around 514 nm, attributed to the photoluminescence of the L-CPDs, as shown in Fig. 5(i). This indicates that L-CPDs maintain high fluorescence stability in the presence of other fluorescent substances. The fluorescence response of L-CPDs throughout the displacement process is shown in Fig. 5(j). From 17 min to 40 min, the fluorescence intensity of L-CPDs gradually increases. Between 40 min until 70 min (when tracer injection is stopped), the fluorescence intensity of L-CPDs tends to stabilize. This variation is attributed to the change in L-CPDs concentration from breakthrough to stabilization. After 70 min, continuous injection of API brine solution was maintained for 140 min. During the period from 70 to 85 min, there was no significant decrease in the fluorescence intensity of L-CPDs.

However, after 85 min, the fluorescence intensity of L-CPDs gradually declined over time. This is attributed to the larger areal sweep efficiency generated by L-CPDs during displacement, causing widespread distribution of L-CPDs within the pores of the microfluidic chip. The changes in the blue, green, and orange fluorescent regions in Fig. 5(c)-(e) confirm this result. Furthermore, a quantitative detection model (Fig. S11) was employed to perform quantitative analysis of L-CPDs in the solution depicted in Fig. 5(j). The accumulated weight of L-CPDs over time is shown in Fig. 5(i). Calculation results indicate a recovery rate of 97% for L-CPDs. The higher recovery rate indicates that they are less likely to be adsorbed by rocks during practical use, resulting in minimal residuals. This ensures the accuracy of monitoring results while also reducing consumption and lowering operational costs. The above test results indicate that L-CPDs have the potential to replace existing fluorescent tracer products, demonstrating promising prospects in the field of oilfield tracers.

In conclusion, we propose a novel method combining top-down and bottom-up approaches to synthesize L-CPDs from lignite, demonstrating their potential as oilfield tracers. These L-CPDs exhibit green fluorescence with an absolute QY of 19.78% and maintain good fluorescence stability at 200 °F in API brine. Quantitative detection models based on the correlation between fluorescence intensity and concentration show that L-CPDs have real-time detection potential with a detection limit of 1 ppb. Furthermore, L-CPDs exhibit low cytotoxicity, minimizing environmental risks. Microfluidic chip experiments visually illustrate the tracing behavior of L-CPDs and confirm their feasibility as alternatives to existing tracer products. The performance of L-CPDs surpasses that of other reported carbon dot-based tracers. The straightforward and cost-effective preparation process makes L-CPDs suitable for industrial-scale production, holding promises for advancing the development of oilfield tracers.
